questions.prompt: What's the strongest pattern to prevent cross-user data leaks?

choices.text: Row-level security at the database (or equivalent storage-layer enforcement). Application filtering depends on every query being written correctly; RLS makes wrong queries physically impossible. App-level scoping is the floor; storage-level enforcement is the safety net.

questions.explanation: Application-only scoping is fragile — one buggy query and you have a cross-user leak. RLS (or equivalent enforcement at the storage layer) makes the leak impossible by construction. Cheap to add; catches the bugs you didn't anticipate.

questions.prompt: User submits GDPR deletion request. Your DB delete is soft-delete. What's still wrong?

choices.text: Soft delete doesn't satisfy GDPR or user trust. Real deletion must propagate to derived data: embeddings, summaries in memory layers, indexed content, possibly audit logs (with documented retention exceptions). Build the deletion plumbing as architecture — every storage layer needs a 'really delete' path.
Wait for the user to re-request.

questions.explanation: Real deletion is the GDPR/CCPA bar. It includes derived data — embeddings, summaries, indexed chunks. Soft-delete leaves shadow data that compliance regulators (and users who care) won't accept. Build deletion as a first-class capability across every storage layer.
